title: Java Multithreading
summary: Master Thread lifecycle, synchronization, volatile, wait/notify, and thread-safe patterns in Java.

questions.prompt: What is the correct way to start a new thread from a `Thread` subclass instance `t`?
questions.explanation: `start()` creates a new OS thread and calls `run()` in it. Calling `run()` directly executes in the current thread — no concurrency.

choices: Visibility: every thread reads the latest written value from main memory
questions.explanation: `volatile` ensures visibility — no CPU cache or compiler register caching. It does NOT make `count++` atomic (that's 3 operations).

questions.prompt: What is the expected value of `count` after two threads each call `increment()` 1000 times, where `increment()` does `count++` without synchronization?
questions.explanation: Without synchronization, `count++` is a read-add-write race condition. Both threads can read the same value, both add 1, and one write is lost.

questions.prompt: What does `synchronized` on a method do?
choices: Runs the method in a new thread
Acquires the object's intrinsic lock — only one thread can execute it at a time
Makes the method atomic at CPU level
Prevents the method from being overridden
questions.explanation: `synchronized` on an instance method acquires `this` object's monitor lock. On a static method, it acquires the class object's lock.

questions.prompt: Why must `wait()` always be called inside a `while` loop (not `if`)?
choices: Spurious wakeups — a thread can wake up without being notified, so the condition must be re-checked
questions.explanation: The JVM can wake up a waiting thread without an explicit notify (spurious wakeup). Always recheck the condition after waking.

choices: Two threads waiting for locks held by each other, indefinitely
questions.explanation: Deadlock: Thread A holds lock 1, waits for lock 2. Thread B holds lock 2, waits for lock 1. Neither can proceed. Prevention: always acquire multiple locks in the same order.

choices: Creates 4 threads immediately and runs 4 tasks
Creates a pool of 4 reusable threads — tasks queue up when all are busy
questions.explanation: A fixed thread pool reuses threads. New tasks queue in a `LinkedBlockingQueue` when all threads are busy. Better than creating a new `Thread` per task.

questions.prompt: What happens if you call `pool.shutdown()` vs `pool.shutdownNow()`?
choices: shutdown() waits for running tasks to finish; shutdownNow() interrupts running threads and returns queued tasks
questions.explanation: `shutdown()` is graceful — no new tasks accepted, running tasks finish. `shutdownNow()` is forceful — interrupts running threads via interrupt().

questions.prompt: Thread A acquires lock on `obj1` then tries `obj2`. Thread B acquires lock on `obj2` then tries `obj1`. What happens?
choices: The JVM detects and resolves it automatically
questions.explanation: Classic deadlock scenario. Java does NOT automatically resolve deadlocks — the threads freeze forever. Fix: enforce lock ordering (always acquire obj1 before obj2).

questions.prompt: Which are valid Thread states in Java?
questions.explanation: Java Thread.State enum: NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED. SLEEPING and IDLE are not Java thread states.

questions.prompt: What is the difference between `notify()` and `notifyAll()`?
choices: notify() wakes one random waiting thread; notifyAll() wakes all waiting threads
notify() broadcasts to all threads in the JVM
questions.explanation: Prefer `notifyAll()` in most cases — safer because `notify()` might wake the wrong thread. `notifyAll()` ensures the right thread eventually re-evaluates its condition.

choices: Two threads running at the same speed
Outcome depends on relative timing/interleaving of threads — non-deterministic result
A thread that finishes before another
A synchronized method that is too slow
questions.explanation: A race condition occurs when correctness depends on timing. The classic example: read-modify-write without synchronization.

questions.prompt: Which of the following makes `count++` thread-safe without synchronized?
questions.explanation: `AtomicInteger.incrementAndGet()` uses CPU-level compare-and-swap (CAS) instructions — atomic without a lock. `volatile` alone doesn't make `++` atomic.

choices: Pauses the current thread for at least 1000ms, releasing CPU but NOT releasing locks
Releases all held locks for 1 second
questions.explanation: `sleep()` pauses the thread but retains any locks it holds. This is different from `wait()` which releases the lock.

choices: A variable accessible by all threads
Each thread has its own independent copy of the variable
A variable that can only be written by one thread
A variable stored on the heap
questions.explanation: `ThreadLocal<T>` provides thread-isolated storage. Each thread sees its own copy — no synchronization needed. Common use: per-request state in web servers.

choices: Decouples the task from thread lifecycle; class can still extend another class
questions.explanation: Java is single-inheritance. `Runnable` lets your class extend a business class AND be scheduled on a thread. Extending `Thread` wastes your one inheritance slot.

choices: A thread that runs with root privileges
A background thread that JVM kills when all non-daemon threads finish
A thread that never throws exceptions
questions.explanation: Daemon threads (e.g., GC thread) exist to serve others. Set via `t.setDaemon(true)` before start(). JVM exits when only daemon threads remain.

choices: Visibility (ensures value written by one thread is seen by another)
questions.explanation: `synchronized` provides both visibility (happens-before relationship) and mutual exclusion (atomicity). It does NOT prevent deadlocks and can reduce performance.

choices: Causes the calling thread to wait until the target thread terminates
questions.explanation: `t.join()` blocks the calling thread until `t` finishes. Used to wait for results from a thread before proceeding.

choices: When you need full-map locking (e.g., iterating + modifying atomically); ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads/writes but iteration may not be fully consistent
questions.explanation: `ConcurrentHashMap` uses segment-level locks for high throughput. `synchronizedMap` locks the entire map per operation. Use `synchronizedMap` only when you need atomic compound operations on the whole map.

flashcards.back: `start()` creates a new OS thread and calls `run()` in it. `run()` directly executes in the current thread — no new thread. Always call `start()`.

flashcards.front: volatile — what it does and doesn't do
flashcards.back: DOES: guarantees visibility (latest value from main memory, no caching). DOESN'T: make compound operations (count++) atomic. Use AtomicInteger for that.
Always acquire multiple locks in the same global order. Thread A: lock(obj1) then lock(obj2). Thread B: lock(obj1) then lock(obj2). Never reverse the order.

flashcards.front: wait() must be in while, not if
flashcards.back: Spurious wakeups: JVM can wake a thread without notify(). Always: `while (!condition) wait()`. The condition must be re-checked after every wakeup.

flashcards.front: Thread pool sizing rule of thumb
flashcards.back: CPU-bound tasks: pool size ≈ CPU cores. I/O-bound tasks: pool size = cores × (1 + wait_time/compute_time). Too many threads → context-switch overhead.
`sleep()`: pauses thread, KEEPS locks, requires no monitor. `wait()`: pauses thread, RELEASES lock, must be called inside synchronized block. Both throw InterruptedException.
Per-thread state without synchronization. Classic uses: database connection per thread, user session in web framework, SimpleDateFormat (not thread-safe). Set via `tl.set(val)`, get via `tl.get()`.
AtomicInteger: lock-free using CAS hardware instruction. Faster for single-variable increments. synchronized: heavier lock, needed for multi-variable atomic operations.

project.brief: Design a thread-safe producer-consumer task queue in Java. Producers submit tasks (Runnable). Consumers pull and execute tasks. Requirements: bounded queue (max 10 tasks), producers block when full, consumers block when empty, graceful shutdown (drain queue then stop). Design the class structure: fields, constructors, methods. Identify every point where synchronization is needed and why. Note which operations must be atomic. You don't need to write running code — map out the design decisions.

checklist.text: Identify shared mutable state and the synchronization primitive for each
Design put() and take() with correct wait/notifyAll logic in while loops
Design shutdown() that drains the queue before stopping consumer threads
Identify 2 potential bugs (e.g., calling run() instead of start(), spurious wakeup, deadlock scenario) and explain how your design avoids them

hints: The queue, a `running` flag, and the count are shared state — each needs careful handling.
For shutdown: set `running=false`, then `notifyAll()` so blocked consumers can check the flag.
Use `while (queue.isEmpty() && running) wait()` in take() — not `if` — to handle spurious wakeups.
Consider using `java.util.concurrent.BlockingQueue` and explaining why it's preferable to a hand-rolled solution.
